title.en: Projects, Folders & IAM: GCP's Resource Hierarchy

description.en: Learn how Google Cloud organizes everything into an Organization > Folders > Projects hierarchy, and how IAM roles, principals, and policy inheritance decide who can do what.

story_variants.en:
## The Filing Cabinet That Runs Your Cloud

Every resource you will ever create in Google Cloud — a VM, a bucket, a Vertex AI endpoint — lives inside a **project**. There are no orphan resources. The project is the unit of billing attachment, API enablement, quota, and (most importantly) access control. Above projects sit **folders**, and above folders sits the **organization** node, which is created automatically when your company signs up with Cloud Identity or Google Workspace.

This hierarchy is not decorative. IAM policies attached at any level **inherit downward**, and the effective policy on a resource is the **union** of the policy set on the resource and every policy above it. There is no 'deny at a lower level cancels an upper grant' in basic IAM — if someone has `roles/editor` at the org node, they have it on every project underneath, full stop (IAM Deny policies exist as a separate, explicit mechanism, but plain role bindings only ever add access).

> **Callout:** Inheritance is additive. You cannot 'un-grant' an inherited role by editing a child project's policy. Fix over-broad grants where they were made — at the top.

## The Three Nouns of IAM

Every IAM decision involves three things:

- **Principal (member)** — who is asking. Formats matter and are graded in real audits: `user:asha@example.com`, `group:data-eng@example.com`, `serviceAccount:etl@proj.iam.gserviceaccount.com`, `domain:example.com`.
- **Role** — a named bundle of permissions, like `roles/storage.objectAdmin`. Permissions themselves (e.g. `storage.objects.get`) are never granted directly.
- **Resource** — the thing the policy is attached to: org, folder, project, or an individual resource such as a bucket.

A **binding** ties a role to a list of principals, optionally with a **condition** (an expression like `request.time < timestamp(...)` or a resource-name prefix). The full set of bindings on a resource is its **IAM policy**.

## Role Types: Know Which One You're Granting

| Role type | Example | Use in enterprise? |
|---|---|---|
| Basic (primitive) | `roles/owner`, `roles/editor`, `roles/viewer` | Avoid. Thousands of permissions across all services. |
| Predefined | `roles/bigquery.dataViewer`, `roles/run.invoker` | Default choice. Curated by Google per service. |
| Custom | `projects/p/roles/deployBot` | When predefined roles are too broad; you maintain them. |

Basic roles predate the modern IAM system. `roles/editor` on a project quietly includes permission to act on nearly every service, which is why org-policy guardrails and security reviews flag it. The enterprise habit is: **predefined roles, granted to groups, at the lowest level that works.**

> **Pitfall:** Granting roles to individual user accounts creates unauditable sprawl. Grant to Google Groups managed by your identity team; membership changes then don't require IAM changes.

## Service Accounts: The Identity of Your Code

Workloads don't log in with passwords — they run **as** a service account. Two directions to keep straight:

- A service account is a **principal**: you can grant it `roles/bigquery.jobUser` so your Cloud Run service can run queries.
- A service account is also a **resource**: you grant a human `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` **on the service account** so they may deploy code that runs as it. This is how privilege escalation gets controlled — being editor on a project doesn't automatically let you impersonate its most powerful robot.

Modern guidance is to avoid downloading long-lived **service account keys** entirely. On GCP compute, credentials come from the metadata server automatically. From GitHub Actions or other clouds, use **Workload Identity Federation**, which exchanges an external OIDC token for short-lived Google credentials — no key file to leak.

## Designing the Hierarchy

A common enterprise layout:

- **Organization: example.com**
  - Folder `shared-infra` — networking host projects, logging sinks
  - Folder `prod` — one project per workload (`api-prod`, `ml-serving-prod`)
  - Folder `nonprod` — matching dev/staging projects
  - Folder `sandbox` — per-team experimentation with budget caps

Why per-workload projects? Because the project is the blast-radius boundary: quotas, API enablement, billing attribution, and IAM all scope there. A compromised dev sandbox should never share a project with production data.

Decision guidance when you're unsure where to bind a role:

- Needed by everyone in the company (e.g. org-wide viewer for a security team)? Bind at the **org**.
- Needed across one environment (all prod projects)? Bind at the **folder**.
- Needed by one workload? Bind at the **project**.
- Needed on one bucket or dataset only? Bind on the **resource** itself.

> **Callout:** Use the Policy Troubleshooter and Policy Analyzer in the console when access 'mysteriously' works — nine times out of ten it's an inherited binding from a folder somebody forgot about.

## What You'll Build

In the practice challenge you'll write a miniature IAM resolver: given a policy's bindings, answer the question every auditor asks — *which roles does this principal actually hold?* It's the mental model behind `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` and the Policy Analyzer, distilled into a dictionary walk. Master this and every later lesson — Cloud Run invoker roles, BigQuery dataset ACLs, Vertex AI service agents — becomes an application of the same three nouns.

description.en: IAM policies attached at any node inherit down every branch below it.

left.label: Grant roles/editor to a user at the org node

right.label: Grant roles/bigquery.dataViewer to a group on one project

description.en: Predefined roles, granted to groups, at the lowest level that works — the enterprise default.

explanation.en: Bindings are edited on a specific resource — here the project api-prod.
The principal uses a typed prefix; group: means membership is managed by the identity team, not IAM.
A predefined role bundles just the BigQuery read permissions — no storage, no compute.

description.en: The IAM policy is just a document of bindings — your challenge simulates resolving it.

stages: Check permission in any granted role

description.en: How Google Cloud evaluates every API call, billions of times a day.

instruction.en: Build a miniature IAM binding resolver — the logic behind Policy Analyzer. Implement roles_for(member, bindings) that returns a SORTED list of role names in which the member appears. Then, for each member in the given order (user:asha@example.com, group:analysts@example.com, user:nobody@example.com), print exactly one line: '<member> -> <role1>, <role2>' with roles sorted alphabetically and joined by ', ', or '<member> -> NO ACCESS' if the member holds no roles. Print nothing else.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
BINDINGS = {
    "roles/viewer": ["user:asha@example.com", "group:analysts@example.com"],
    "roles/bigquery.dataViewer": ["group:analysts@example.com"],
    "roles/editor": ["user:dev@example.com"],
    "roles/storage.objectAdmin": ["serviceAccount:etl@proj.iam.gserviceaccount.com", "user:asha@example.com"],
}

def roles_for(member, bindings):
    # TODO: return a sorted list of role names whose member list contains `member`
    pass

MEMBERS = ["user:asha@example.com", "group:analysts@example.com", "user:nobody@example.com"]

for m in MEMBERS:
    # TODO: print '<member> -> role1, role2' (roles sorted, comma+space separated)
    #       or '<member> -> NO ACCESS' when the list is empty
    pass
practice_challenges.expected_output:
user:asha@example.com -> roles/storage.objectAdmin, roles/viewer
group:analysts@example.com -> roles/bigquery.dataViewer, roles/viewer
user:nobody@example.com -> NO ACCESS

en: Loop over bindings.items() and collect the role name whenever member is in the members list.
Use sorted() on the collected role names before returning — string sort puts roles/bigquery... before roles/viewer.
Use ', '.join(roles) for the output line, and an if/else for the NO ACCESS case.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert roles_for("user:asha@example.com", BINDINGS) == ["roles/storage.objectAdmin", "roles/viewer"]
assert roles_for("group:analysts@example.com", BINDINGS) == ["roles/bigquery.dataViewer", "roles/viewer"]
assert roles_for("user:nobody@example.com", BINDINGS) == []
assert roles_for("serviceAccount:etl@proj.iam.gserviceaccount.com", BINDINGS) == ["roles/storage.objectAdmin"]

q.en: A user has roles/editor granted at the organization node. What access do they have on a project three levels below?

en: None until the role is re-granted on the project
roles/editor — IAM policies inherit downward and are additive
Only roles/viewer, since inheritance downgrades roles
It depends on the project's billing account

q.en: Which role type should be your default choice in an enterprise environment?
Why does granting roles/iam.serviceAccountUser ON a service account matter?

en: It lets the service account send email
It controls which humans may run workloads AS that service account, limiting privilege escalation
It is required before the service account can be billed
It enables the IAM API in the project

q.en: Your CI system on GitHub needs GCP access. What is the recommended 2025-era approach?

en: Download a service account JSON key and store it as a repo secret
Use Workload Identity Federation to exchange the OIDC token for short-lived credentials
